name: Union, States, and Union Territories

sourceRefs.label: 2025_q page 48 Q50 (Union Territory High Courts; Arunachal Pradesh/Mizoram statehood 1987, with answer key)
2024_q page 40 Q58 (Match enacted-year to Act: Union Territories Act 1963, GNCTD Act 1991, National Honour Act 1971, Representation of People Act, with answer key)
Articles 1-4 text, Part VIII/Article 239/239AA/241 — standard civics facts

researchNotes: 2 real past-paper hits found via OCR grep for 'union territory/formation of states/article 1/article 3' — both unusually rich (a true/false statement-combination question and a 4-item match-the-following on enacted years), giving solid confidence this clause is actively tested. Cross-checked both against actual constitutional facts before trusting the marked answer key: confirmed Delhi DOES have its own High Court (so the exam's 'false' statement claiming otherwise is correctly false), and confirmed all 4 Act-year pairings (1963/UT Act, 1991/GNCTD Act, 1971/National Honour Act, 1951/Representation of People Act) against real enactment dates.

reading.intro: Covers Articles 1-4 (the Union and its territory, formation of new states) and Part VIII (Union Territories). Real exam evidence shows TNPSC tests this via statement-combination ('which of the following are true') and match-the-following (Act name vs. year of enactment) formats specifically.

qa.question: What does Article 1 of the Constitution declare about India?
qa.answer: "India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States" — establishing India as an indestructible Union, not a voluntary federation that states can secede from (unlike the original US model).

qa.question: What was the ORIGINAL (1949) four-fold classification of states, and what replaced it?
qa.answer: Part A states (former Governor's Provinces of British India), Part B states (former princely states/unions of princely states), Part C states (centrally administered, including former Chief Commissioners' Provinces), and Part D (the Andaman & Nicobar Islands). This was abolished by the States Reorganisation Act, 1956 along with the 7th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1956.

qa.question: Who chaired the States Reorganisation Commission, and what did it recommend vs. what was actually implemented?
qa.answer: Justice Fazal Ali chaired the Commission (members: H.N. Kunzru, K.M. Panikkar), constituted December 1953, report submitted September 1955. It recommended 16 states + 3 centrally administered territories, primarily reorganising states on a LINGUISTIC basis — but the States Reorganisation Act, 1956 that Parliament actually passed created 14 states + 6 Union Territories.

qa.question: What do Articles 2, 3, and 4 empower Parliament to do?
qa.answer: Article 2: admit into the Union, or establish, new states. Article 3: form new states by separation/uniting territory from existing states, alter boundaries, or change names of existing states. Article 4: laws made under Articles 2/3 can amend the First Schedule (list of states/UTs) and Fourth Schedule (Rajya Sabha seat allocation) without being treated as a formal Constitutional Amendment.

qa.question: Is a law under Article 3 (altering state boundaries/names) passed as an ordinary law or a Constitutional Amendment, and what is the procedure?
qa.answer: As an ORDINARY law, by simple majority — NOT a Constitutional Amendment under Article 368. However, the Bill must first be referred by the President to the affected State Legislature(s) for their views (not their consent — Parliament can override their views) within a specified time, before introduction.

qa.question: How are Union Territories administered, per Part VIII of the Constitution?
qa.answer: Under Article 239, every Union Territory is administered by the President, acting through an Administrator appointed by him/her (titled Lieutenant Governor in some UTs). Some UTs (e.g. Puducherry, and Delhi specially) have been given their own Legislative Assembly and Council of Ministers by separate laws — they are not all administered identically.

qa.question: Real exam: Does Delhi have its own High Court? Is Parliament empowered to constitute a High Court for any Union Territory?
qa.answer: Yes — Delhi DOES have its own separate High Court (the Delhi High Court, established 1966) — a real exam question that frames this as FALSE (claiming Delhi has no separate High Court) is testing recognition of that false statement. Separately, Article 241 DOES empower Parliament to constitute a High Court for any Union Territory by law — both this and the Delhi-HC fact were tested together in one real combination question.

qa.question: Real exam: When did Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram become full-fledged states, and which Act for Union Territories' administration governs many of the rest?
qa.answer: Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram became full-fledged states on 20 February 1987 (under the State of Arunachal Pradesh Act and the State of Mizoram Act, both 1986). Separately, the Government of Union Territories Act, 1963 provides for Legislative Assemblies/Councils of Ministers in certain Union Territories.

qa.question: Real exam: Match these enacted years to their Acts — 1963, 1991, 1971, 1951.
qa.answer: 1963 → Government of Union Territories Act. 1991 → Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi Act (gave Delhi its special Article 239AA legislative status). 1971 → Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act. 1951 → Representation of the People Act (note: there are two — the 1950 Act on delimitation/voter qualification, and the 1951 Act on conduct of elections; the exam's pairing uses 1951).

qa.question: What does 'Union of States' (Article 1) imply that a voluntary federation would not?
qa.answer: That states have no right to secede and cannot voluntarily leave the Union — a deliberate design ensuring an indestructible Union created by the Centre, rather than states voluntarily banding together with a right to withdraw.

qa.question: What special territorial provision governs Jammu & Kashmir today, following the 2019 abrogation of Article 370?
qa.answer: Under the Jammu and Kashmir Reorganisation Act, 2019, J&K was reorganized into two Union Territories: Jammu & Kashmir (with its own legislature) and Ladakh (without a legislature).

qa.question: Which Union Territories have their own Legislative Assembly, and which don't?
qa.answer: Delhi and Puducherry have Legislative Assemblies, granted by special laws under Articles 239AA/239A respectively. Most other UTs (Chandigarh, Lakshadweep, Andaman & Nicobar Islands, Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu) do not, and are administered directly by an Administrator.

questions.prompt: Article 1 of the Constitution declares that India, that is Bharat, shall be a:
The States Reorganisation Commission (1953-55) was chaired by:
The States Reorganisation Act, 1956 created how many states and Union Territories, compared to what the Commission had recommended?
options: 14 states + 6 UTs, vs. the Commission's recommended 16 states + 3 UTs
questions.explanation: Act created 14 states + 6 UTs; Commission had recommended 16 states + 3 UTs.

questions.prompt: Which Article empowers Parliament to alter the boundaries, area, or name of an existing State by an ordinary law (simple majority, not a Constitutional Amendment)?
Before introducing an Article 3 Bill altering a State's boundary, the President must refer it to:
options: The Supreme Court for binding consent
The affected State Legislature(s) for their views
No one — it can be introduced directly
questions.explanation: The affected State Legislature(s), for views (not binding consent).

questions.prompt: Under Part VIII of the Constitution, a Union Territory is administered by the President acting through:
options: The Speaker of the State Legislature
The Chief Justice of the High Court

questions.prompt: Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? (i) Parliament is empowered to constitute a High Court for any Union Territory. (ii) Delhi does not have a separate High Court of its own. (iii) Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram became full-fledged states in 1987.
questions.explanation: (i) and (iii) are true; (ii) is false — Delhi DOES have its own High Court.

questions.prompt: Match: (a) Government of Union Territories Act, (b) Government of NCT of Delhi Act, (c) Prevention of Insults to National Honour Act, (d) Representation of the People Act — with years 1963, 1991, 1971, 1951 respectively. Which option correctly lists (a)(b)(c)(d) in year-order 1963/1991/1971/1951?
questions.explanation: The Acts pair exactly with the years as listed: 1963/UT Act, 1991/GNCTD Act, 1971/National Honour Act, 1951/Representation of the People Act.

questions.prompt: Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram became full-fledged states of the Indian Union on:
The Government of National Capital Territory of Delhi Act, which gave Delhi its own Legislative Assembly and Council of Ministers, was enacted in:
Declaring India a 'Union of States' (Article 1) implies that Indian states:
options: Can voluntarily secede at any time
Cannot voluntarily secede from the Union
Must reapply for membership every decade
questions.explanation: States cannot voluntarily secede — an indestructible Union.

questions.prompt: Following the 2019 abrogation of Article 370, Jammu & Kashmir was reorganized into which two Union Territories?
Which of these Union Territories has its own Legislative Assembly?
